questions.a: A virtual filesystem exposing kernel and process information.
Resource accounting and limits for groups of processes.
To isolate views of system resources such as PIDs, mounts, and networks.
A Linux init system and service manager.
Basic file permission bits for owner, group, and others.

questions.q: What is a capability in Linux?
questions.a: A fine-grained privilege bit that splits root powers into smaller pieces.
It exposes live kernel and process state without special tooling.

questions.q: Why do namespaces matter for containers?
questions.a: They make a process believe it has its own isolated system view.

questions.q: Why are capabilities safer than full root access?
questions.a: They grant only the specific privilege needed instead of all administrator powers.
Restricting the set of system calls a process may make.

questions.q: What role do SELinux and AppArmor play?
questions.a: They add mandatory access control policies on top of Unix permissions.

questions.q: Why does systemd replace older init scripts in many distros?
questions.a: It provides dependency-aware service management, supervision, and boot orchestration.

questions.q: When would you use a namespace without cgroups?
questions.a: When you want isolation of view but do not need explicit resource limits.

questions.q: What tradeoff does strict MAC policy introduce?
questions.a: It improves security, but policy mistakes can block legitimate behavior and slow operations work.

questions.q: Why can kernel introspection be both powerful and dangerous?
questions.a: It helps diagnose issues deeply, but it can expose sensitive state or encourage invasive changes.

questions.q: How do capabilities and namespaces complement each other in containers?
questions.a: Namespaces isolate the environment, while capabilities limit what the process can do inside it.

questions.q: What is the tradeoff of using systemd units for everything?
questions.a: You gain centralized control and observability, but config can become more complex.

questions.q: Why do Linux internals matter when debugging production issues?
questions.a: They explain how the kernel is actually scheduling, isolating, and limiting the workload.
